sound_type: electric buzzing throb -- tymbal vibration, completely different from stridulation, 100+ dB, the loudest insect on Earth, E=4, forest edge habitat, THIRD CONSECUTIVE INSECT (87-88-89)
ecosystem: Forest edge. Tree trunks and branches. Transition zone between forest and meadow. Arboreal.
season: Summer (peak calling during hot days). Annual emergence.

species_pair.pair_type: Third consecutive insect (87-88-89). The insect trio. Three different sound mechanisms: stridulation (cricket/katydid) vs tymbal vibration (cicada). Two orders: Orthoptera (87-88) and Hemiptera (89).

coordinates.description: Reference for forest edge habitat in the Puget Sound lowlands, Washington. Cicada calls audible in wooded edges and transition zones.

taxonomy_note.description: THIRD CONSECUTIVE INSECT (87-88-89). NEW FAMILY Cicadidae (40th -- MILESTONE!). NEW ORDER Hemiptera. Different from Orthoptera (cricket/katydid at 87-88). Tymbal mechanism (not stridulation). Family-level entry. Annual cicadas.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Cicadidae from Latin cicada ('cicada, tree cricket'). Hemiptera from Greek hemi ('half') + pteron ('wing'), referring to the half-hardened forewings.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Family-level entry. Multiple genera contribute to PNW forest-edge soundscapes. Dog-day cicadas and related annual species.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Hemiptera -- NEW ORDER. True bugs, cicadas, aphids, leafhoppers. Different from Orthoptera (87-88). Second insect order in the engine.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Insecta -- third entry (87, 88, 89). Fourth class in the engine. The insect trio.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Cicadidae's FIRST entry at degree 89. NEW FAMILY (40th -- MILESTONE). Forty families in 89 degrees.

song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 3,000-6,000 Hz. Broad peak from tymbal harmonics.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. TYMBAL VIBRATION. Rapid buckling of paired abdominal membranes at 100-500 cycles per second.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual call: seconds to minutes of continuous buzzing. Chorus: hours during peak summer heat.
song_metrics.note_structure: Continuous buzzing drone. Tymbal clicks fuse into a sustained electric throb. Some species modulate intensity (pulsing). No discrete notes.
song_metrics.note_types: Calling buzz (primary advertisement, attracts females), disturbance squawk (produced when handled), courtship modification (quieter, directed).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: No phrase structure. Continuous drone. Some species have on-off pulsing patterns, but the basic signal is sustained.
song_metrics.timbre: Electric. Buzzing. Throbbing. Sounds more like a power line or electronic oscillator than a biological organism. Harsh, metallic quality.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Individual cicadas exceed 100 dB at close range. The LOUDEST INSECT ON EARTH. Collective chorus fills entire forest edge. Absolute acoustic dominance.
song_metrics.tempo: Continuous. No rhythm in the cricket sense. Some species pulse, but the basic sound is a sustained drone.
song_metrics.context: BREEDING CALL: males vibrate tymbals to attract females. Diurnal. Peak calling in heat of day. Summer. The collective chorus is the primary acoustic phenomenon.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Species composition varies by region. PNW annual cicadas differ from eastern periodical cicadas. Multiple species may contribute to any given chorus.
song_metrics.key_property: ELECTRIC BUZZING THROB -- the 90th acoustic element. Third insect sound. TYMBAL VIBRATION -- completely different from stridulation. 100+ dB. Loudest insect. E=4. Forest edge. The most powerful insect sound in the engine.

physical_description.overall: Family-level entry. Annual cicadas: 25-50 mm body length, robust body, large head with prominent compound eyes, transparent wings held tent-like over body. Green, brown, or black coloration depending on species. Males have paired tymbal organs on sides of abdomen.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 89: (1) TYMBALS -- paired vibrating membranes on abdomen (males). (2) LARGE COMPOUND EYES -- prominent, widely spaced. (3) TRANSPARENT WINGS -- held tent-like, veined. (4) ROBUST BODY -- stocky. (5) SOUND -- most often detected by the electric buzz, not by sight.
physical_description.skin_detail: EXOSKELETON: Chitinous exoskeleton. Green, brown, or black with markings. Wings transparent with visible venation. Discarded nymphal exoskeletons (exuviae) found clinging to tree bark after emergence.
physical_description.field_id: Most often detected by SOUND -- the electric buzzing throb on summer days. Large, robust insects on tree trunks. Discarded exoskeletons on bark. The sound is unmistakable. Listen for the electric drone.

nodes.title: Third Consecutive Insect -- The Insect Trio (87-88-89)
nodes.description: Cricket (87), katydid (88), cicada (89). Three consecutive insects. Three different sound mechanisms. Two orders (Orthoptera, Hemiptera). Comprehensive insect acoustic exploration.

nodes.title: Electric Buzzing Throb -- 90th Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Tymbal vibration. 100+ dB. Loudest insect on Earth. Electric, buzzing, throbbing drone. E=4. Completely different from stridulation. Forest edge.

nodes.title: Cicadidae -- 40th Family (MILESTONE!) -- New Order Hemiptera
nodes.description: NEW FAMILY (40th -- milestone). Forty families in 89 degrees. NEW ORDER Hemiptera. Different from Orthoptera. Second insect order.

nodes.title: Tymbal Vibration -- New Sound Mechanism
nodes.description: Paired abdominal membranes buckled by muscles. 100-500 cycles per second. Clicks fuse into continuous drone. Hollow abdomen amplifies. Fundamentally different from stridulation.

nodes.title: Loudest Insect on Earth -- 100+ dB
nodes.description: Individual cicadas exceed 100 dB. Louder than a motorcycle. Tymbal mechanism with abdominal resonating chamber. Absolute acoustic dominance.

nodes.title: The Parenthetical Girls Parallel
nodes.description: Both dramatic and overwhelming. Cicada fills forest edge with electric throb. Parenthetical Girls fills venue with theatrical intensity. Both maximalists in their domains.

nodes.title: Twenty-Ninth Consecutive Original CSV
nodes.description: Twenty-nine consecutive original CSV species. No substitution. Original.

cross_references.relationship: INSECT TRIO (87-88-89). Cricket = stridulation (chirp). Cicada = tymbal vibration (throb). Different mechanisms. Both E=4. Meadow (cricket) vs forest edge (cicada). Three insects, three mechanisms.
CONSECUTIVE INSECTS (88-89). Katydid = wing stridulation (continuous buzz, Orthoptera). Cicada = tymbal vibration (electric throb, Hemiptera). Three consecutive, three mechanisms, two orders.
ELECTRIC BUZZING QUALITY. The Varied Thrush's ethereal buzzy whistle has an electric quality reminiscent of the cicada's tymbal drone. Different phyla, similar timbral impression.
SUSTAINED SOUND. Both produce continuous, sustained sounds that fill their soundscapes. Beaver gnaws persistently; cicada throbs persistently.
CHORUS PARALLEL. Both overwhelming collective sounds. Treefrog (marsh, nocturnal) and cicada (forest edge, diurnal). Both E=4. Four consecutive non-mammalian entries (86-87-88-89).
